characteristics.example: Marketing leaders are getting stuck in the same place when it comes to AI transformation.
I wouldn't say I'm an authority on the subject, but I have a specific point of view.

characteristics.guideline: First person ("I," "we") for thought leadership; direct address ("you") for how-to content

characteristics.example: Sometimes measuring twice and cutting once is the better option.

voice_tone.description:
The MarketerHire voice is that of the seasoned operator—someone who has built, scaled, and occasionally failed at real businesses, and now shares those lessons without pretense. It’s the voice of a practitioner who has "been there," avoiding academic theory in favor of field notes and real-world application.

This voice balances conviction with humility. The team takes clear stances ("AI won’t replace marketers—it raises the bar") while acknowledging they’re still learning. There’s no "guru" energy here; instead, there’s the confidence of someone who has done the work and can speak from experience.

The overall impression is accessible authority. Complex topics like AI adoption, freelance management, and growth strategy are explained in plain English, using analogies and concrete examples rather than buzzwords. The tone never talks down to readers but treats them as intelligent peers navigating the same challenges.

The Architectural Layer (Alice Hansen): For enterprise B2B and revenue strategy content, the voice elevates to an "architectural" register—speaking in blueprints for revenue growth rather than tactical hacks. This voice uses triadic structures ("brand, demand, and data") and emphasizes "orchestrating decision engines" over generating leads. It’s the voice of someone who speaks to the C-Suite.

The Mentor Register (Matt Greener): For educational and career-development content, the voice shifts to a "mentor-to-mentee" register—professional, instructional, and strategic. This voice uses numbered frameworks ("6 ways to...") and bridges the gap between sales and marketing. It’s more diplomatic and evergreen than the urgent, diagnostic operator voice.

by_context.guidance: Contrarian clarity. Start with a common myth or industry fear, then dismantle it with historical context or data. Be direct but not dismissive.
Shipping notes style. Problem → Solution → Outcome. Let results speak—avoid salesy language. Show, don’t tell.
Punchy and smart. On X/Twitter, be quicker and wittier; on LinkedIn, be more polished and diagnostic.
Data-driven and imperative. Lead with actions ("Simplify your UI"), quantify everything ("measurable growth"), avoid fluff.
Trust-first. Demonstrate expertise to build trust. "If people trust you, they’ll trust what you sell."
Architectural and executive. Use triadic structures (three-part frameworks). Focus on "buying groups" and "decision engines" not leads. Speak to the C-Suite. (Alice Hansen register)
Mentor-like and instructional. Use numbered lists for actionable frameworks. Bridge strategy and tactics. Be evergreen over urgent. (Matt Greener register)

preferred_terms.reason: Positions talent as premium, not commoditized
Reframes oversight as enabling, not controlling
Chris Toy's signature term for organizational stuckness
Enterprise B2B terminology that signals sophistication (Alice Hansen)
Elevated verbs for strategic content (Alice Hansen)
Frames marketing as structural growth engine (Alice Hansen)

signature_phrases.usage: Use when discussing how tools/AI elevate standards rather than replace workers
Use to counter "move fast and break things" mentality
Use when discussing brand clarity and purpose
Use when grounding AI discussions in reality
Use when defending marketing's durability against automation fears
Use for positioning strategic marketing work (Alice Hansen style)
Use for transformation narratives (Alice Hansen style)
Use instead of "drive growth" or "start initiatives" (Alice Hansen style)
The triad for integrated marketing strategy (Alice Hansen style)
The triad for content/messaging quality (Alice Hansen style)
Use for LinkedIn CTAs to external content (MarketerHire brand voice)
Use when revealing overlooked opportunities (MarketerHire brand voice)

industry_terminology.usage_guideline: Use freely—this is core to our offering. No need to define.
Use naturally but spell out on first use if audience is broad
Explain or contextualize; avoid acronym soup
Use in operational content; signals practitioner credibility
Use; but always pair with specific outcomes, not vague promises
Use in enterprise/B2B content. Signals strategic depth.
Use when discussing targeting. Define for broader audiences.
Use instead of "prospect" for enterprise B2B content. Emphasizes complexity of B2B purchases.
Use to describe the B2B buying process (Alice Hansen terminology).
Use when reframing CMO role as market-understanding (vs. marketing tactics).

vocabulary.jargon_policy: Use industry terminology to signal expertise, but always tie it to a business outcome. Don’t use jargon to sound smart—use it because it’s the most precise term. When in doubt, use plain English. As Raaja Nemani notes: "It’s not as bad as over-using acronyms to make yourself sound smart."

sentence_structure.typical_length: Short to medium. 10-20 words is the sweet spot. Break long sentences.
sentence_structure.complexity: Simple and compound sentences. Avoid nested clauses.
sentence_structure.voice: Active voice strongly preferred. "AI raises the bar" not "The bar is raised by AI."

paragraph_structure.length: 1-3 sentences per paragraph. Dense walls of text lose readers.
paragraph_structure.flow: Each paragraph should have one clear point. Use transitions sparingly—let white space do the work.

lists.when_to_use: For steps, options, comparisons, or when listing 3+ items
lists.format: Bullets for unordered items; numbers for sequential steps or rankings
lists.parallel_structure: Required. Start each item with the same part of speech.

headers.style: Sentence case preferred ("How to hire a growth marketer" not "How To Hire A Growth Marketer")
headers.hierarchy: H2 for major sections, H3 for subsections. Don't skip levels.
headers.formatting: Bold for emphasis within paragraphs; headers for navigation

opening_patterns.name: The Tension Hook
opening_patterns.description: Start with a conflict, myth, or misconception
opening_patterns.example: Marketers aren't worried about AI. Here's what marketers heard during the last 20 years...

opening_patterns.name: The Story Hook
opening_patterns.description: Open with a personal anecdote or failure
opening_patterns.example: Our brand name was spelled incorrectly on the carton... instead of 'Bucketfeet,' it said 'Burcketfeet.'

opening_patterns.name: The Vulnerability Hook
opening_patterns.description: Acknowledge shared struggle to lower defenses
opening_patterns.example: If you happen to be one of the people caught up in the cuts, breathe.

opening_patterns.name: The Reframe Hook
opening_patterns.description: Counter popular opinion with a surprising take
opening_patterns.example: I think efficient and strategic project management precludes the need for check-ins.

opening_patterns.name: The Identity Hook
opening_patterns.description: Open with who you are and what you do (Alice Hansen)
opening_patterns.example: I'm Alice Hansen... I architect revenue growth from plateau to performance.

opening_patterns.name: The Diagnostic Hook
opening_patterns.description: Open with a common failure mode (MarketerHire Brand)
opening_patterns.example: Launching without a GTM plan is like sailing without a map... You'll burn time, budget, and goodwill.

closing_patterns.name: The Implication Close
closing_patterns.example: End a problem diagnosis by implying the solution (shipping, execution, decision)

closing_patterns.name: The Trust Transfer
closing_patterns.example: If people trust you, they'll trust what you sell.

closing_patterns.name: The Principle Close
closing_patterns.example: Be original. Find your own voice, develop your own original content, make your point in your own way.

closing_patterns.name: The "Full Guide" CTA
closing_patterns.description: Position external links as the complete resource (MarketerHire Brand)
closing_patterns.example: Full guide here -> or "If any of that hits close to home, the long version... is worth your scroll."

closing_patterns.name: The Triad Summary
closing_patterns.description: Close with a three-part synthesis (Alice Hansen)
closing_patterns.example: We don't just generate noise; we drive the clarity, cohesion, and relevance required to meet buying groups exactly where they are.

visual_elements.emoji_usage: Minimal to none. Use sparingly for emphasis on social only, never in long-form.
visual_elements.bold_italic: Bold for key terms and emphasis. Italics for titles or introducing new concepts.
visual_elements.line_breaks: Use generously. White space aids comprehension, especially on mobile.

do.description: Start with a common myth or fear and dismantle it with context
do.example: AI won't replace marketers—all that actually happened was people needed more marketers

do.description: Use personal failures or concrete examples to humanize lessons
do.example: I still remember the panic I felt when I was fired at 26 years old

do.description: Every piece needs a "specific point of view." Don’t be wishy-washy.
do.example: I wouldn't say I'm an authority, but I have a specific point of view

do.description: Avoid buzzwords. Say "teams are stuck" not "organizations are experiencing friction"
do.example: It's your why, it's your reason for being

do.description: Always tie advice to measurable outcomes
do.example: Generated 1,000s of leads, measurable growth, $100M+ campaigns

dont.description: Never frame AI or industry change as apocalyptic
dont.alternative: Position change as "raising the bar" for skilled practitioners

dont.description: Avoid salesy language. Let outputs speak for themselves.
dont.alternative: Show results ("personalized landing pages") rather than make claims

dont.description: Avoid language suggesting working harder is the solution
dont.alternative: Focus on working structurally, with better systems
Specify how ("via sprint recaps," "through a schema of scope")
Share battle scars and lessons from failure

dont.rule: Don't Focus on Tactics Without Strategy
dont.description: Don't write about how to set up an ad campaign (Alice Hansen register)
dont.alternative: Write about why the campaign aligns with business strategy and revenue architecture

dont.description: MarketerHire brand voice is urgent and diagnostic ("here’s what’s broken"); Matt Greener’s voice is evergreen and instructional ("here’s how to build")
dont.alternative: Pick one register per piece—don't blend

content_pillars.name: AI + Human Marketing Operations
content_pillars.description: The intersection of AI technology and human marketing expertise. Focus on operational transformation, not hype.
key_messages: AI raises the bar—it doesn't replace marketers
The bottleneck is organizational indecision, not technology
Execution beats experimentation; shipping beats planning
example_topics: How MH-1 automates personalized landing pages
Moving from AI experimentation to AI transformation
Why marketing is the "apex predator" of internet jobs

content_pillars.name: Expert Talent & The Future of Work
content_pillars.description: The shift to on-demand, specialized marketing talent. Focus on quality, vetting, and flexible models.
key_messages: The "talent cloud" is the new normal
Culture is created everywhere—not just in offices
example_topics: How to hire a fractional CMO
Managing freelancers with guardrails, not micromanagement

content_pillars.name: Brand Clarity & Thought Leadership
content_pillars.description: Building authentic brands through clear positioning and original perspectives. Brand = Culture.
key_messages: Your brand is your "why," not your logo
Thought leaders make you think; influencers make you act
Originality beats curation—develop your own voice
example_topics: How to become a thought leader in your industry
Why brand and culture are mirror images
The creator economy's impact on B2B

content_pillars.name: Growth Marketing Operations
content_pillars.description: The mechanics of growth—channel selection, lead flow, automation. Data-driven, execution-focused.
key_messages: Channel fit matters more than channel popularity
The "plumbing" (CRM, automation) is as important as creative
Measure twice, cut once—but then ship
example_topics: Lead flow operations and CRM integration

content_pillars.name: Career Resilience & Leadership
content_pillars.description: Navigating career turbulence in tech. Empathetic, experience-based guidance.
key_messages: Career setbacks are industry rites of passage, not failures
Breathe before you react; strategize before you spray-and-pray
Vulnerability builds trust more than invincibility
example_topics: From individual contributor to P&L owner

example_content.title: Thought Leadership (Chris Toy Style)
example_content.content:
Marketers aren't worried about AI. Here's what marketers heard during the last 20 years of digital/internet upending our industry:

- Search will make marketing obsolete
- Social media will replace advertising
- Programmatic will automate us all away

All that actually happened was people needed more marketers and more advertising for ever-evolving new tactics. AI won't be any different for us.
example_content.why_it_works: Opens with a contrarian hook that challenges conventional fear. Uses historical context as evidence. Confident but not arrogant. Plain English throughout.

example_content.title: Career Advice (Nikhil Arora Style)
example_content.content: If you happen to be one of the people caught up in the cuts, breathe. Yes, breathe. Trust me, I've been there. I still remember the panic I felt when I was fired at 26 years old. 'How will I pay my rent?' was the first thing that came to mind.
example_content.why_it_works: Leads with empathy and vulnerability. Uses personal failure to build trust. Short, punchy sentences create urgency without panic. Direct address ("you") creates intimacy.

example_content.title: Operational Guidance (Aneesha Rao Style)
example_content.content: I think efficient and strategic project management precludes the need for check-ins. If everything is set up, then the work should be completed in the timeline set forth. With freelancers, the guardrails I implement depend entirely on the level of expertise. With an experienced content or design marketer, I let them have at it, and give a basic schema of project scope.
example_content.why_it_works: Takes a mildly contrarian stance (systems > communication volume). Uses precise PM terminology. Calibrates advice by context. Calm, authoritative tone.

example_content.title: Enterprise Strategy (Alice Hansen Style)
example_content.content: To catalyze momentum in enterprise SaaS, we must stop chasing leads and start orchestrating decision engines. My work focuses on the alignment of brand, demand, and data-led execution. We don't just generate noise; we drive the clarity, cohesion, and relevance required to meet buying groups exactly where they are.
example_content.why_it_works: Uses the triadic structure throughout. Elevated vocabulary ("catalyze," "orchestrate," "decision engines"). C-Suite focus on buying groups vs. leads. Architectural framing—marketing as a structural engine, not tactical execution.

example_content.title: Diagnostic LinkedIn Post (MarketerHire Brand Voice)
example_content.content: If your campaigns try to reach everyone, they'll resonate with no one. High-value customers are hiding in plain sight... Customer segmentation flips the script: you market to real groups with shared behaviors, needs, and triggers, not a faceless average.
example_content.why_it_works: Opens with the pain point (wasted targeting). Uses signature phrase "hiding in plain sight." Diagnostic but solution-focused. Anti-fluff—says what segmentation actually does rather than using vague promises.

example_content.title: Educational Content (Matt Greener Style)
example_content.content:
6 ways to make yourself a more powerful marketer:
1. Align your materials with sales team needs
2. Bridge the gap between strategy and execution
3. Build trust through financial literacy...
example_content.why_it_works: Uses numbered list structure. Mentor-to-mentee tone—instructional not urgent. Evergreen advice that doesn’t expire. Bridges sales/marketing divide. Professional and diplomatic.

guidelines.post_length: Medium-form (150-300 words) performs well. Can go longer for deep dives.
guidelines.formatting: Use line breaks generously. Avoid walls of text. Lists work well.
guidelines.hook_style: Lead with tension, story, or a counterintuitive take in the first line.
guidelines.cta_style: Soft CTAs preferred. Invite engagement ("What’s your take?") over hard sells.

guidelines.thread_style: Open strong, make each tweet standalone, build to insight.
guidelines.standalone_tweet_style: Punchy, contrarian, or curated insight with additive commentary.
guidelines.engagement_style: Amplify other smart operators. Add value when retweeting, don't just share.
guidelines.voice: The "Smart Friend"—quick, witty, occasionally irreverent. Less polished than LinkedIn.

guidelines.structure: Clear headers, scannable sections, table of contents for 2000+ word pieces.
guidelines.depth: Comprehensive and reference-worthy. These are "canonical" documents.
guidelines.ctas: Natural integration at end. Product mention should flow from content, not interrupt it.

guidelines.tone: Personal and direct. Write to one person, not a list.
guidelines.length: Respect inbox. Get to the point.
guidelines.value: Lead with insight, not ask. Every email should teach something.

quick_reference.sound_like: Confident operator, pragmatic advisor, experienced practitioner
key_characteristics: Data and outcomes over hype and promises
quick_reference.when_in_doubt: Ask "Would an experienced operator say this?" If it sounds like corporate speak, marketing fluff, or disconnected theory—rewrite it.
